steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage establishes a hard fact: no source before 1155 includes references to the Round Table. Annales Cambriae was composed around 970 — well before 1155. The inference must follow necessarily from this timeline, not introduce new claims about content, accuracy, or availability of sources.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Malory did not use Annales Cambriae as a source for information when he presented about the Round Table.': Annales Cambriae predates 1155, so it contains no Round Table material. A source with no Round Table content cannot be Malory's source for Round Table information. This inference follows directly from the passage's stated rule. ✓
  • Choice B — 'Geoffrey of Monmouth’s accounts of Arthurian legends in his History are more similar overall in content to the accounts in the Mabinogion than they are to the accounts in Roman de Brut.': The passage says nothing about the overall content similarity among these works. This is an unsupported comparison. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Geoffrey of Monmouth was unaware of stories of the Round Table when composing his History, though historians know that works containing such stories were available to him.': This directly contradicts the passage — it implies pre-1155 works with Round Table stories existed, which the passage explicitly denies. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Le Morte d’Arthur is more historically accurate than History, because the Mabinogion had not been written when Geoffrey of Monmouth was writing his work.': The passage makes no judgment about historical accuracy, and the Mabinogion dating claim is fabricated — the passage places it in the 12th and 13th centuries, overlapping with Geoffrey's 1130s work. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
